evidence_quotes.quote: The problem is that they do not say that they sell limited licences to use their games, but that they sell games (goods – product).
They end the game’s functionality by closing down their servers.

This could achieve virtually zero costs by changing a pair of code lines so that players themselves can host servers for such a game or disable the online functionality of such a game so as not to block the possibility of playing ‘solo’ on their own machine (console or PC).

paragraphs.text: Hello, I would like to suggest that a clause be added to this act to protect the rights of consumers of digital goods. Specifically, computer games. At present, producers of computer games can ‘kill’ some of the games they have sold to consumers by using plain language. The functioning of any game does not in fact require access to the manufacturer’s servers via an internet connection. In fact, game manufacturers offer limited licences to use their product and have no obligation to inform customers when they end the game’s functionality by closing down their servers. The problem is that they do not say that they sell limited licences to use their games, but that they sell games (goods – product). These games are often not a pure digital good and have physical copies – boxes containing a DVD disk (or a pair of discs) with files to be installed. And these physical purchases suddenly cease to function with the will of the producers over time. To be clear: I do not suggest that the manufacturers of those games maintain servers for those games for all. This would have been arbitrarily and impossible. However, I would like to offer players the continued functionality of the games before closing down their servers. This could achieve virtually zero costs by changing a pair of code lines so that players themselves can host servers for such a game or disable the online functionality of such a game so as not to block the possibility of playing ‘solo’ on their own machine (console or PC). Currently, producers of such games do not want to introduce such solutions on the basis that consumers who will be given the opportunity to launch purchased games will be forced to ‘buy’ a new game. No commercial branch has such absolute and absurd power over its products. For comparison, please imagine a person who buys a new car simply not even from the salon but from the factory. Abruptly after 2-3 weeks, this person cannot get to the car. The key does not work, the wheels are locked and the panty plates have fallen on the glazing, preventing them from being assembled and entering the centre. This person now has a useful smelter, which he cannot even sell, because nobody will be interested in buying, because everyone knows that it cannot be activated. That was the fate of Concord, developed by Firewalk Studios and published by Sony Interactive Entertainment. It can be physically bought copies with disks. You can install gigabytes of her files on your computer. No one will do so because the game cannot be launched because it requires connection to Sony servers, which switched them off a few days after the game was published. A game for which people cost hundreds of zlotys. I therefore warmly ask you to add to this act thought-out regulatory requirements on manufacturers of computer games. Thank you for your attention and apologise for your inconclusive contribution.
